section.title: Isaac Born, Hagar Sent
section.sectionRationale: After twenty-five years of waiting, the promise arrives: Sarah bears Isaac, the son of laughter, exactly as God had said. But the joy is soon crossed by conflict, as Ishmael is sent away. The chapter shows God keeping His word to the letter and caring even for the son who is not the heir.
section.thesis: God visits Sarah and does exactly what He promised, turning barren laughter into joy; yet the arrival of the son of promise forces a painful separation, in which God both narrows the line to Isaac and hears the cry of Hagar and Ishmael in the desert.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the birth of Isaac and Sarah's laughter of joy; the conflict that leads to Hagar and Ishmael being sent away; and God's provision for the boy in the wilderness.

verses.text: Now the LORD {{paqad|attended}} to Sarah as He had said, and the LORD did for Sarah what He had promised.
So Sarah conceived and bore a son to Abraham in his old age, at the very time God had promised.
And Abraham gave the name Isaac to the son Sarah bore to him.
When his son Isaac was eight days old, Abraham circumcised him, as God had commanded him.
Abraham was a hundred years old when his son Isaac was born to him.
Then Sarah said, “God has made me {{tsachaq|laugh}}, and everyone who hears of this will laugh with me.”
She added, “Who would have told Abraham that Sarah would nurse children? Yet I have borne him a son in his old age.”
So the child grew and was weaned, and Abraham held a great feast on the day Isaac was weaned.
But Sarah saw that the son whom Hagar the Egyptian had borne to Abraham was mocking her son,
and she said to Abraham, “Expel the slave woman and her son, for the slave woman’s son will never share in the inheritance with my son Isaac!”
Now this matter distressed Abraham greatly because it concerned his son Ishmael.
But God said to Abraham, “Do not be distressed about the boy and your maidservant. Listen to everything that Sarah tells you, for through Isaac your offspring will be reckoned.
But I will also make a nation of the slave woman’s son, because he is your offspring.”
Early in the morning, Abraham got up, took bread and a skin of water, put them on Hagar’s shoulders, and sent her away with the boy. She left and wandered in the Wilderness of Beersheba.
When the water in the skin was gone, she left the boy under one of the bushes.
Then she went off and sat down nearby, about a bowshot away, for she said, “I cannot bear to watch the boy die!” And as she sat nearby, she lifted up her voice and wept.
Then God {{shama|heard}} the voice of the boy, and the angel of God called to Hagar from heaven, “What is wrong, Hagar? Do not be afraid, for God has heard the voice of the boy where he lies.
Get up, lift up the boy, and take him by the hand, for I will make him into a great nation.”
Then God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water. So she went and filled the skin with water and gave the boy a drink.
And God was with the boy, and he grew up and settled in the wilderness and became a great archer.
And while he was dwelling in the Wilderness of Paran, his mother got a wife for him from the land of Egypt.

paqad.gloss: To visit, attend to, take note of and act. When God attends to Sarah, it means He turns His active care toward her to fulfill His word. The same verb marks moments when God intervenes to keep His promises, here bringing life to a barren womb at the appointed time.

tsachaq.gloss: To laugh. The name Isaac, Yitschaq, means he laughs, and this root has run through the story: Abraham laughed, Sarah laughed in doubt, and now she laughs for joy. God turns the laughter of unbelief into the laughter of praise, and invites all who hear to laugh with her.

shama.gloss: To hear, listen, heed. God heard the voice of the boy, echoing the meaning of Ishmael, God hears. The word ties this rescue to the earlier promise in chapter 16, showing that the God who hears the afflicted keeps hearing, even the outsider He did not choose as the covenant heir.

basic.context: After twenty-five years, God gives Abraham and Sarah the promised son, Isaac, exactly when He said. Joy soon meets conflict when Ishmael mocks and Sarah demands he be sent away. The key question is how God can be faithful to the child of promise and still care for the son who is not the heir.
basic.hermeneutics: This is history that fulfills a long-standing promise, and the point is God's exact faithfulness. The sending away of Hagar is painful, and the text names Abraham's distress, but God directs and comforts. Readers debate the harshness of the expulsion, yet God's care for the boy is unmistakable.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with God attending to Sarah, a word for God stepping in to act on His promise. Isaac's name means he laughs, and the laughter of doubt now becomes laughter of joy. In the desert God hears the boy, echoing the name Ishmael, which means God hears.
basic.typology: Isaac, the child of promise born by God's power to the as-good-as-dead, points to the miracle of grace and to the true Son. Paul reads Isaac and Ishmael as children of promise and of flesh, a picture of the two covenants.
basic.christ: Isaac, the long-awaited son who brings laughter and is born against all natural hope, foreshadows Christ, the promised Son through whom joy comes to the world. Paul says believers, like Isaac, are children of promise, born of the Spirit.
basic.god: God is faithful to the letter of His word, doing exactly what He promised at the very time He named. He is also compassionate, hearing the cry of an outcast boy and providing water and a future.
basic.crossReferences: Galatians 4 reads Isaac and Ishmael as two covenants. Romans 9 says through Isaac the offspring is reckoned. Hebrews 11 recalls the faith that received the promised son.
basic.oneStory: The son of promise finally arrives, proof that God keeps His word against every impossibility, and the line narrows to Isaac. Yet God still hears the outsider. The story presses on toward Christ, the ultimate promised Son, in whom the children of promise are born and the nations are blessed.

context.literary: The chapter resolves the long tension of Sarah's barrenness with a burst of joy and wordplay on laughter, then immediately introduces new conflict. It deliberately parallels chapter 16: again Hagar is in the desert, again the angel of God speaks, again God hears, but now the child is Ishmael himself.
context.historical: The inheritance rights of a slave woman's son versus a wife's son were a live legal issue in the ancient world; Sarah's demand concerns Isaac's undivided inheritance. Weaning, celebrated with a feast, typically came around age two or three, marking a child's survival of the perilous early years.
context.canonical: Isaac's birth fulfills the promise stretching back to Genesis 12 and becomes the anchor of the line to Christ, cited in Romans 9. Paul develops the Isaac and Ishmael contrast into a full allegory of the two covenants in Galatians 4, freedom and slavery, promise and flesh.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the exact fulfillment of God's promise in Isaac's birth, to narrow the covenant line to Isaac while showing God's compassion for Ishmael, and to display a God who keeps His word and hears the afflicted.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The events, including the painful expulsion of Hagar, are described and interpreted by God's own words, not held up as a model of family conduct. The prescriptive truths are God's faithfulness to His promise and His compassion toward the outcast.

debates.question: Was the sending away of Hagar and Ishmael just, given its harshness?

views.label: A God-directed narrowing of the covenant line
views.case: God Himself tells Abraham to listen to Sarah, for through Isaac the offspring is reckoned, and He promises to make Ishmael a nation; the separation serves the covenant purpose while providing for Ishmael.

views.label: A painful human failure God then redeems
views.case: The scene shows real cruelty and Abraham's grief, and some stress that God works good out of a harsh, sinful situation rather than commending the expulsion itself.

debates.bearing: Both agree God preserves the line through Isaac and shows genuine mercy to Hagar and Ishmael; the passage holds together election and compassion.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with the LORD paqad, attending to or visiting Sarah, a verb for God turning His active care to fulfill His word. The name Isaac, Yitschaq, he laughs, gathers up the whole thread of tsachaq that has run through the story, and Sarah's doubting laugh becomes joyful praise: God has made me laugh. In the desert God shama, heard, the voice of the boy, echoing the name Ishmael, God hears, so that the God who heard Hagar's affliction in chapter 16 is shown hearing still.

typology.type: Isaac, the promised son born by God's power
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the promised Son born in the fullness of time, and all believers as children of promise, Galatians 4:28.

typology.type: Laughter turned from doubt to joy
typology.fulfillment: The joy of salvation, mourning turned to laughter, that God gives His people, Psalm 126:2.

typology.type: Isaac and Ishmael, promise and flesh
typology.fulfillment: Paul's allegory of the two covenants, the free woman and the slave, fulfilled in Christ, Galatians 4:22-31.

study.christ: Isaac is the long-promised son, born against all natural possibility to bring laughter and joy, and through his line comes the ultimate promised Son, Jesus, in whom joy comes to the whole world. Paul takes Isaac as the pattern of every Christian: now we, brothers, like Isaac, are children of promise, born not according to the flesh but through the Spirit, Galatians 4:28-29. And the God who hears the dying boy in the desert is the same God who hears and saves through Christ, leaving no cry of the afflicted unheard.
study.god: God is meticulously faithful, doing for Sarah exactly what He said, at the very time He had named, bringing life from a barren, aged womb. He is sovereign in choosing to reckon the offspring through Isaac. And He is compassionate, hearing the voice of an outcast boy, opening a mother's eyes to water, and promising to make even Ishmael a great nation, for he too is Abraham's offspring.

crossReferences.note: Paul reads Isaac and Ishmael as an allegory of the two covenants, promise and slavery.
Through Isaac your offspring will be reckoned; the children of promise are counted as offspring.
By faith Sarah received power to conceive, and from one as good as dead came descendants beyond counting.
The earlier desert scene where God first heard Hagar, echoed as God again hears the boy.

study.oneStory: The son of promise finally arrives, proof that God keeps His word against every impossibility, and the covenant line narrows to Isaac. Yet even as the story focuses on the chosen heir, God still hears the cry of the outsider and provides. The line runs on toward Christ, the ultimate promised Son, in whom the children of promise are born and, at last, all the nations are blessed.

quiz.q: How does the chapter emphasize God's faithfulness in Isaac's birth?

options.text: God did for Sarah exactly what He had promised, at the very time He had said.
options.note: The text stresses precise fulfillment of God's word.

options.text: Isaac was born much sooner than God had promised.
options.note: He was born at the appointed time, not sooner; the point is exactness.

options.text: Isaac was born through ordinary, natural circumstances.
options.note: The birth was a miracle to an aged, barren couple, not ordinary.

options.text: God's promise was only partly fulfilled.
options.note: The promise was fully and exactly kept.

quiz.q: What does the name Isaac mean, and how does it fit the story?

options.text: He laughs, gathering up the laughter of doubt now turned to joy.
options.note: Sarah says God has made her laugh, turning earlier doubt into praise.

options.text: God hears, marking that God heard Sarah's prayer.
options.note: That meaning belongs to Ishmael; Isaac means he laughs.

options.text: Father of many, celebrating Abraham's descendants.
options.note: That is the meaning of Abraham's name, not Isaac's.

options.text: God provides, recalling the gift of water.
options.note: Isaac means he laughs; provision is a theme, not the name's meaning.

quiz.q: How did God respond when Hagar and Ishmael were dying in the desert?

options.text: He heard the boy's voice, comforted Hagar, and provided a well of water.
options.note: God's hearing echoes the meaning of Ishmael's name.

options.text: He ignored them because Ishmael was not the heir.
options.note: God was not the covenant line through Ishmael, yet He heard and provided.

options.text: He sent them back to Abraham's household.
options.note: He provided for them in the wilderness rather than sending them back.

options.text: He let the boy die as judgment.
options.note: God saved the boy and promised to make him a nation.

quiz.q: Why did God tell Abraham to listen to Sarah about sending Ishmael away?

options.text: Because through Isaac his offspring would be reckoned, narrowing the covenant line.
options.note: God directs the line of promise through Isaac while still blessing Ishmael.

options.text: Because Ishmael had no future at all.
options.note: God promised to make Ishmael a great nation; he did have a future.

options.text: Because Abraham no longer cared about Ishmael.
options.note: The text says Abraham was greatly distressed over his son.

options.text: Because Sarah was acting against God's will.
options.note: God confirmed Sarah's word as serving His covenant purpose.

quiz.q: How does Paul use Isaac in the New Testament?

options.text: As the pattern of believers who are children of promise, born through the Spirit.
options.note: Galatians 4 makes Isaac a picture of the covenant of promise.

options.text: As an example of salvation earned by works.
options.note: Isaac pictures promise and grace, not works.

options.text: As a warning against having children in old age.
options.note: That misses Paul's allegory of the two covenants entirely.

options.text: As proof that the promise failed.
options.note: Isaac is proof the promise succeeded, born exactly as God said.

questions: God did exactly what He promised, at exactly the time He said, after twenty-five years. Where are you waiting on a promise of God, and how does Isaac's birth encourage you?
Sarah's laughter of doubt became laughter of joy. Has God ever turned something you doubted into a cause for praise?
God heard the cry of an outcast boy in the desert. Who are the overlooked people in your world whose cries God is calling you to notice?
The coming of the promised son brought both joy and painful conflict. How do you hold on to God's faithfulness when His blessings arrive tangled with hard circumstances?
Paul says believers are children of promise, like Isaac, born by the Spirit. How does it change your sense of identity to know you belong to God by promise, not by your own effort?

translationNotes.note: Attended to translates a verb meaning to visit or take note and act; it signals God's active intervention to keep His word to Sarah.
God has made me laugh plays on the name Isaac, he laughs; the same root has carried doubt and now carries joy.
Mocking may also be rendered playing or laughing; some manuscripts read mocking her son, and the word again echoes the laughter theme, here turned hostile.
